title: Page 4
text:
CHAPTER 1: INTRODUCTION 
1.1 Background: Processor architectures have long been dominated by proprietary ISAs — 
ARM charges millions in licensing fees and per-chip royalties, while x86 is not licensed to third 
parties at all. This restricts innovation, increases cost, and creates geopolitical dependency for 
nations building critical systems. 
1.2 RISC-V and the FU540: RISC-V is a free, open-source ISA created at UC Berkeley in 
2010, maintained by RISC-V International with 3,000+ member organisations. The SiFive 
FU540 is the first physical chip to implement RISC-V at application-processor level, running full 
Linux distributions and proving RISC-V is viable beyond academia. 
1.3 Objectives: This report studies the FU540's ISA, pipeline, memory hierarchy, privilege 
levels, interrupt handling, real-world applications, and India's contribution through the SHAKTI 
processor. 
 
CHAPTER 2: LITERATURE REVIEW 
2.1 RISC-V ISA: The RISC-V specification defines a minimal base integer ISA (RV32I/RV64I, 
47 instructions) with optional modular extensions. The G shorthand combines IMAFD 
extensions. The FU540 implements RV64GC — the minimum required to boot Linux. 
2.2 Prior Work: Before the FU540, RISC-V existed only in FPGAs and microcontrollers 
(FE310 — no MMU, no Linux). The FU540 was the first to implement a full MMU, multi-core 
design, and DDR controller in commercial RISC-V silicon. 
2.3 Comparison with ARM Cortex-A72: The ARM Cortex-A72 (Raspberry Pi 4) uses a 3-
wide out-of-order pipeline at 1.8 GHz on 16nm — delivering 3–4× higher single-thread 
performance than the FU540. However ARM imposes significant licensing costs and prohibits 
ISA modification, while RISC-V is zero-cost and fully open. 
2.4 SHAKTI — IIT Madras: India's SHAKTI processor, developed at IIT Madras under Prof. 
V. Kamakoti since 2014, is built on RISC-V and taped out at SCL Chandigarh on 180nm. ISRO

title: Page 5
text:
adopted it for satellite systems requiring full design transparency, radiation tolerance, and zero 
foreign dependency. 
 
 
CHAPTER 3: SYSTEM OVERVIEW / THEORY 
3.1 RISC-V ISA — Base and Extensions: RV64I provides 32 general-purpose 64-bit registers 
and 47 integer instructions. Extensions add capability: M (multiply/divide), A (atomic operations 
for multi-core), F (32-bit float), D (64-bit float), C (compressed 16-bit instructions). The FU540 
implements RV64GC — full general-purpose ISA with compressed instructions, sufficient to run 
Linux. 
3.2 Instruction Formats: All base instructions are fixed 32-bit width across six formats — R 
(register-register), I (immediate/load), S (store), B (conditional branch, 13-bit PC-relative offset), 
U (upper immediate, LUI/AUIPC), J (unconditional jump, JAL, 21-bit offset). Register fields rs1 
and rs2 are at fixed bit positions across all formats, enabling parallel register read and decode. 
3.3 Register File: 32 general-purpose registers — x0 (hardwired zero), x1/ra (return address), 
x2/sp (stack pointer), x3/gp (global pointer), x4/tp (thread pointer), x5–x7/t0–t2 (temporaries), 
x8–x9/s0–s1 (saved), x10–x17/a0–a7 (function arguments and return values), x18–x27/s2–s11 
(saved), x28–x31/t3–t6 (temporaries). 
3.4 FU540 SoC Overview: Four U54 application cores (RV64GC, 1 GHz, full MMU, 32KB L1 
I+D cache each) and one E51 monitor core (RV64IMAC, M-Mode only, no MMU). All cores 
share 2MB L2 cache via TileLink coherent interconnect. Peripherals include UART, SPI, I2C, 
GPIO, GbE MAC, PLIC, CLINT, PCIe ×4, and DDR4 controller (8GB on HiFive Unleashed 
board). 
3.5 Memory Hierarchy: Three-level hierarchy — L1 (32KB private per core, 1-cycle), L2 
(2MB shared, ~10 cycles), DDR4 RAM (8GB, ~60–100 cycles). Cache stores 64-byte lines 
exploiting spatial locality. Cache hit serves in 1 cycle; L2 miss causes 60–100 cycle pipeline 
stall. TileLink maintains coherency across cores using MESI protocol.

title: Page 6
text:
3.6 Privilege Levels: Three levels — Machine (M-Mode, mandatory, highest privilege, 
OpenSBI firmware), Supervisor (S-Mode, Linux kernel), User (U-Mode, applications). 
Transitions via ecall (lower→higher) and mret/sret (higher→lower). Key CSRs: mstatus, mtvec, 
mepc, mcause. 
 
CHAPTER 4: WORKING PRINCIPLE 
4.1 Datapath and Load-Store Architecture: The processor datapath flows left to right — PC 
→ Instruction Memory → Control Unit → Register File → ALU → Data Memory → Register 
File. All computation is register-to-register. Memory is accessed exclusively through LOAD 
(LW — Load Word, memory→register) and STORE (SW — Store Word, register→memory) 
instructions. This keeps the pipeline clean and each stage simple. 
4.2 Five-Stage Pipeline: Instructions flow through five sequential stages — IF (fetch instruction 
from L1 I-cache using PC), ID (decode opcode, read rs1/rs2 from register file simultaneously), 
EX (ALU performs operation or computes memory address), MEM (LOAD reads / STORE 
writes L1 D-cache, others pass through), WB (result written to destination register rd). In steady 
state, all five stages operate in parallel on different instructions — one instruction completes 
every clock cycle. 
4.3 Pipeline Hazards and Forwarding: Data hazards occur when an instruction needs a result 
not yet written back. Resolved by data forwarding — routing the result directly from EX or 
MEM stage output back to EX stage input, bypassing the register file. If forwarding is 
insufficient (load-use hazard one cycle too early), a pipeline stall (bubble) is inserted. The 
FU540's U54 core implements forwarding in hardware. 
4.4 Interrupt and Exception Handling: Four hardware stages — (1) Triggered: current 
instruction completes, MIE bit checked, PLIC arbitrates external interrupts; (2) Context Saved: 
mepc ← PC, mstatus saves privilege (MPP) and interrupt enable (MPIE), MIE cleared to 0, 
mcause written with cause code; (3) Handler Executes: PC jumps to mtvec, handler reads 
mcause and services the request; (4) Execution Resumes: mret/sret atomically restores PC from 
mepc, privilege from MPP, and interrupt enable from MPIE.

title: Page 7
text:
4.5 Boot Sequence: E51 wakes first → executes Mask ROM → loads OpenSBI from SPI Flash 
→ initialises DDR/PLIC/CLINT → releases U54 cores → U-Boot loads Linux from MicroSD 
→ Linux kernel boots → user shell prompt. 
 
CHAPTER 5: APPLICATIONS 
•  Embedded Linux Boards: HiFive Unleashed runs Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian — targets 
lightweight servers, gateways, and industrial Linux systems.  
•  Space and Defence: ISRO uses SHAKTI (RISC-V) in satellites — open ISA enables 
radiation hardening and eliminates foreign export dependency.  
•  AI Accelerators: NVIDIA (GPU management), Esperanto Technologies, and Tenstorrent use 
RISC-V for AI chip control processors, adding custom vector extensions.  
•  Storage Controllers: Western Digital ships 1 billion RISC-V cores annually in HDDs and 
SSDs — eliminating ARM royalties at high volume.  
•  Security Chips: Google Titan M2 (Pixel 6+) uses RISC-V — fully auditable ISA prevents 
hidden backdoors in a chip protecting encryption keys.  
•  IoT Devices: ESP32-C3 brings RISC-V to millions of embedded engineers — minimal RV32I 
cores consume milliwatts for sensors and smart devices.
 
CHAPTER 6: ADVANTAGES AND LIMITATIONS 
Advantages 
•  Zero licensing fees — no royalties for implementation or manufacture  
•  Fully open and auditable — critical for security, space, and defence  
•  Modular and customisable — custom extensions without vendor permission  
•  Geopolitically neutral — Swiss foundation, immune to export restrictions  
•  Linux-capable — full MMU and privilege levels on FU540 
Limitations 
•  Performance gap — in-order pipeline 3–4× slower than ARM Cortex-A72's out-of-
order design  
•  Older process node — 28nm vs ARM's 16nm, higher power at same performance

title: Page 8
text:
•  Ecosystem maturity — toolchain and driver support less mature than ARM's 30-year 
ecosystem  
•  Limited high-performance options — competitive out-of-order RISC-V chips are 
recent and still few.
 
CHAPTER 7: FUTURE SCOPE 
•  Performance: SiFive P670 (2022) competes with ARM Cortex-A75 using out-of-order 
execution — gap closing rapidly.  
•  AI and Edge: RISC-V V extension and custom AI instructions make it the preferred ISA for 
new AI chip designs.  
•  Space: ESA's MICRORISC-V and ISRO's SHAKTI independently converging on RISC-V for 
radiation-hardened satellite processors.  
•  India: C2S program trains 85,000 chip design engineers on RISC-V by 2026 — building a 
self-sustaining domestic semiconductor ecosystem under the ₹76,000 crore National 
Semiconductor Mission.  
•  Mobile and Automotive: Google's Android RISC-V porting project and Bosch/NXP 
automotive RISC-V controllers signal broader mainstream adoption by 2030.
 
CHAPTER 8: CONCLUSION 
The SiFive FU540 demonstrated that a free, open-source ISA can power a real application 
processor running full Linux — marking RISC-V's transition from academic project to industrial 
reality. Its 5-stage pipeline, modular RV64GC ISA, 3-level cache hierarchy, Sv39 virtual 
memory, and three-level privilege architecture collectively form a complete and coherent 
processor design. Beyond the chip itself, RISC-V's zero licensing cost, full auditability, and 
geopolitical neutrality have driven adoption by Google, NVIDIA, Western Digital, and ISRO. 
India's SHAKTI program at IIT Madras represents not just adoption but active contribution — 
designing indigenous RISC-V silicon for national space infrastructure. RISC-V is not simply an 
alternative to ARM — it is a movement toward open, transparent, and sovereign computing.

text:
